skill.name: dnanexus-integration
skill.description: Guides DNAnexus cloud genomics work: app and applet development, dxpy automation, data transfer, workflow execution, and platform configuration.
skill.summary: Build, run, and manage DNAnexus genomics apps, workflows, and data operations.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and sensitive-data patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown examples and DNAnexus terminology. Human review found no prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent, but the skill legitimately guides authenticated cloud data movement, external commands, Docker, network access, project permissions, and destructive operations. Publish with warnings and require user confirmation for credentials, data movement, job execution, and removal actions.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated DNAnexus Data Movement
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for operational risk: the skill teaches upload, download, search, and result retrieval patterns for authenticated DNAnexus projects. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but mishandled prompts could move regulated genomics data or write results to the wrong project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples directly describe cloud file upload, download, and searches across accessible projects. The use is expected for DNAnexus, but the data sensitivity and project scope create real operational risk.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: PARTIAL TRUE POSITIVE: most static Ruby/backtick alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, but the skill does include shell, subprocess, dx CLI, pip, and Docker execution examples. Commands appear instructional rather than malicious, but execution should require review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown documentation, not executable scripts, which lowers malicious confidence. The command and subprocess examples are real and could affect local files, packages, containers, or cloud jobs if executed automatically.

medium_findings.title: Project Permission and Destructive Operation Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for sensitive capability: the references document project invitations, file removal, permanent deletion, and job termination. These are valid DNAnexus operations, but they require explicit user confirmation and least-privilege checks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly cover sharing, removal, deletion, and termination APIs. They are legitimate administrative examples but can cause data loss, access changes, or interrupted analysis.

medium_findings.title: Broad Network and Docker Permission Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for elevated platform risk: configuration examples include Docker execution and broad network access. These are common in bioinformatics workflows, but they increase supply-chain, egress, and reproducibility risks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly request network access and run Docker containers. The same document also advises limiting network permissions, so this is a legitimate but review-worthy pattern.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: scanner hits for weak cryptography and reconnaissance appear to come from DNAnexus terms such as hash input type, object describe methods, and whoami/version checks. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation, password dumping, or host reconnaissance intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are ordinary DNAnexus documentation and introspection examples. I did not find evidence that the skill implements MD5/SHA1 hashing, dumps Windows SAM data, or performs host reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE but low risk: the skill lists DNAnexus documentation, API reference, and GitHub URLs. They are transparent reference links, not hidden exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation resources and match the skill topic. No evidence found of credential or data submission to these links.

low_findings.title: API Token Handling Examples Use Placeholders
low_findings.description: LOW RISK: the Python SDK reference shows token configuration with placeholder values and also warns against hardcoding tokens. Users still need guidance to avoid pasting real tokens into generated files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token examples use placeholders, which reduces immediate risk. The pattern is still sensitive because environment-based authentication can expose secrets if copied into logs or source files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess and Shell Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation shows subprocess, shell, dx CLI, pip install, and Docker commands. These should be treated as commands requiring user review before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are concrete executable patterns inside documentation. They are not hidden code, but automatic execution could alter local and cloud environments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authentication Token Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The SDK reference includes API token and DX_SECURITY_CONTEXT examples. Generated guidance should keep tokens out of source code, logs, and chat transcripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly show bearer token configuration and environment-variable authentication. Values are placeholders, so the risk is handling practice rather than embedded secret leakage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Network and Project Access Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: Configuration examples request internet access, project write access, all-project read access, and developer permissions. These settings should be minimized for production apps.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The broad access settings are explicit in the examples. They may be valid for some DNAnexus apps but require least-privilege review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Data Deletion and Job Termination APIs
dangerous_patterns.description: The references include file removal, permanent deletion, and job termination examples. These operations should require explicit confirmation and project verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited operations can delete data or stop active analyses. They are legitimate administrative APIs but have clear irreversible or disruptive effects.

content.value_statement: DNAnexus development often requires precise platform commands, SDK patterns, and configuration details. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan apps, data operations, and workflows with DNAnexus-specific guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to create and build DNAnexus apps and applets.
Shows dxpy patterns for files, records, projects, jobs, and workflows.
Guides upload, download, search, cloning, and folder organization tasks.
Describes job launching, monitoring, retries, subjobs, and workflow chaining.
Documents dxapp.json inputs, outputs, dependencies, Docker, assets, and access settings.
Provides genomics-oriented examples for FASTQ, BAM, VCF, alignment, and variant workflows.
limitations: Requires a valid DNAnexus account and appropriate project permissions.
Does not execute DNAnexus commands unless the host AI tool is allowed to run them.
Examples use placeholder project, file, applet, and job identifiers.
Users must review cost, privacy, and compliance requirements before running cloud jobs.

use_cases.title: Create a DNAnexus applet
use_cases.description: Design app inputs, outputs, runtime files, and build steps for a new analysis applet.

use_cases.title: Organize sequencing data
use_cases.description: Plan uploads, downloads, metadata, folders, and project permissions for FASTQ or BAM datasets.

use_cases.title: Run and monitor workflows
use_cases.description: Launch analyses, track job states, collect outputs, and diagnose failed DNAnexus jobs.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a simple upload
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me upload local FASTQ files to a DNAnexus project. Ask for the project ID, folder, naming convention, and metadata before suggesting commands.

prompt_templates.title: Draft an applet structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a DNAnexus applet for a single-sample BAM processing step. Include inputs, outputs, dependencies, resource needs, and test strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Build a dxpy automation plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a dxpy-based plan to find all VCF files matching sample metadata, run an applet for each file, and collect result links.

prompt_templates.title: Review a production workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my DNAnexus workflow design for permissions, network access, Docker dependencies, cost risk, data movement, and failure recovery.

output_examples.input: I need to run alignment for 40 paired FASTQ samples on DNAnexus.
output: A workflow outline with upload, metadata, applet selection, job launch, monitoring, and output collection steps.
Questions about project ID, reference genome, instance type, expected outputs, and permission level.
Warnings to confirm cost, protected data rules, and token handling before execution.

output_examples.input: Help me configure a DNAnexus app that uses samtools and a Docker image.
output: A configuration checklist covering run specification, dependencies, Docker access, input and output classes, and instance type.
A review of whether broad network access is necessary.
Testing steps for local logic and platform execution.

output_examples.input: My DNAnexus job failed after running for two hours.
output: A troubleshooting sequence for job state, logs, failure messages, input closure, permissions, and resource limits.
A retry plan that preserves original inputs and records the new job identifier.
Suggestions for improving error handling and runtime estimates.

best_practices: Confirm project IDs, permissions, and data sensitivity before suggesting upload, download, or sharing actions.
Use placeholders for tokens and require users to authenticate through approved DNAnexus methods.
Ask users to approve command execution, destructive operations, broad network access, and costly instance choices.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode DNAnexus API tokens or protected sample identifiers in generated files.
Do not use broad network access or developer permissions unless the workflow clearly requires them.
Do not remove files, terminate jobs, or invite users without explicit confirmation.

faq.question: Does this skill require a DNAnexus account?
faq.answer: Yes. Users need a DNAnexus account, authentication, and permissions for the projects they use.

faq.question: Can it run DNAnexus commands directly?
faq.answer: It provides guidance and examples. Actual command execution depends on the host tool and user approval.

faq.question: Does it support dxpy Python SDK work?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for handlers, file operations, jobs, projects, direct API calls, and errors.

faq.question: Can it help with protected biomedical data?
faq.answer: It can help plan workflows, but users must apply their own privacy, consent, and compliance controls.

faq.question: What file types are covered?
faq.answer: The examples focus on common genomics files, including FASTQ, BAM, VCF, FASTA, GTF, and reports.

faq.question: Why does the audit show elevated risk?
faq.answer: The skill discusses cloud data movement, command execution, authentication, Docker, network access, and destructive operations.
